lede: Good Saturday, NOLA. Today's a quieter day after a huge week, but there's still plenty to dig into. The big threads: Apfel brings free AI to your Mac, Claude Code just found a 23-year-old Linux vulnerability, and Anthropic is making OpenClaw significantly more expensive. We've also got new research on how AI affects human thinking, plus some solid takes on where the vulnerability research field is headed.

quick_links.note: Real-time tracker of MCP (Model Context Protocol) adoption and sentiment. Handy if you're tracking the ecosystem.
quick_links.title: Ismcpdead.com: Live MCP Adoption Dashboard

quick_links.note: GitHub CEO says platform activity is surging — 275M commits per week. The AI agent boom is real.
quick_links.title: GitHub's Insane Growth: 275M Commits Per Week

quick_links.note: A provocative take on AI startups built on shaky foundations. Worth reading if you're evaluating whether your favorite AI company has real moat.
quick_links.title: The Subprime AI Crisis Is Here

quick_links.note: The Verge on the cultural shift: everything looks like AI now, and we're going to need proof systems to verify human authorship. Weird future.
quick_links.title: Really, You Made This Without AI? Prove It

quick_links.note: Internal reorganization at OpenAI. Fidji Simo (AGI deployment CEO) is taking leave; Kate Rouch (CMO) stepping back for health reasons.
quick_links.title: OpenAI Executive Shuffle: Brad Lightcap Gets 'Special Projects' Role

quick_links.note: More detail on the executive moves. Fidji Simo (who was leading AGI deployment initiatives) is stepping back temporarily.
quick_links.title: OpenAI's AGI Boss Takes Leave of Absence

quick_links.note: Transparency issue: child safety orgs were working with what they thought was an independent coalition, but OpenAI was secretly funding it.
quick_links.title: Kids Safety Groups Didn't Know OpenAI Was Backing Their Coalition

quick_links.note: AMD's approach to local inference — using both GPU and NPU for speed. Worth checking out if you're doing edge AI work.
quick_links.title: AMD's Lemonade: Fast Local LLM Server with GPU + NPU

quick_links.note: Linux kernel maintainers are seeing a huge spike in security reports — many AI-generated, some legitimate, all require manual triage. The vulnerability research world is adjusting in real-time.
quick_links.title: Open-Source Vulnerability Research Is Being Flooded with AI-Generated Reports

quick_links.note: Anthropic is getting serious about policy. New PAC to back candidates aligned with the company's agenda. Sign of an industry maturing into politics.
quick_links.title: Anthropic Launches Political Action Committee

quick_links.note: Meta, Google, Microsoft all building massive natural gas infrastructure for AI. Long-term bet on energy or a mistake they'll regret?
quick_links.title: AI Companies Betting Billions on Natural Gas Plants — What Could Go Wrong?

quick_links.note: Polling data on public opinion about AI infrastructure. The data center debate is far from settled.
quick_links.title: People Would Rather Have an Amazon Warehouse Than a Data Center in Their Backyard

items.body: Popular on HN — Apfel is a Mac app that gives you free access to AI models you already have installed locally. No subscriptions, no API keys, no sending data anywhere. If you've been hesitant to try local inference, this is a dead-simple entry point. Works with open-source models like Llama and Mistral.
items.title: Apfel: Free AI Already on Your Mac

items.body: A fresh take on how you structure AI agent projects. ctx is built for developers who want their agents to have better context awareness and reasoning. Think of it as scaffolding for the agent-builder workflow — structured prompts, memory management, and tool orchestration baked in. Discussion here.
items.title: ctx: An Agentic Development Environment

items.body: A handy open-source project that uses AI to help you maximize frequent flyer points and find better travel deals. It's the kind of practical tool that shows how AI can save you real money on something people actually care about. HN discussion.
items.title: Travel Hacking Toolkit: Points Search + Trip Planning with AI

sections.title: New Tools & Things You Can Try

items.body: This is the story that's dominating the vulnerability research world right now. Claude Code spotted a real security flaw in the Linux kernel that humans missed for over two decades — and it did it by reading through old code comments. Simon Willison's take captures the bigger picture: frontier models have fundamentally changed the game for security researchers. HN thread.
items.title: Claude Code Found a Linux Vulnerability Hidden for 23 Years

items.body: Starting today, Anthropic is making it much harder to use OpenClaw (a popular AI agent tool) with Claude on paid plans. Users will need to use a different model or pay extra. This is a notable move: Anthropic essentially deciding that OpenClaw posed enough of a security or policy risk that they'd restrict access rather than ban it outright. Ars Technica's security perspective is worth reading too.
items.title: Anthropic Bans OpenClaw, Makes It Expensive for Claude Users

items.body: New research shows that when people use AI to answer questions, they're surprisingly willing to accept incorrect answers without fact-checking. Large majorities in experiments just... trusted the AI and moved on. It's the kind of finding that should make product builders think hard about how to surface confidence levels, uncertainty, and sources. Not doom — just a design challenge.
items.title: Cognitive Surrender: AI Users Abandon Logical Thinking

items.body: A solid hands-on review of Claude Code's new features and where they shine. If you're building with agents or thinking about upgrading your coding workflow, this gives you the real-world view of what's actually useful versus what's hype.
items.title: How Claude Code's Superpowers Actually Work

items.body: Anthropic's research team dug into how LLMs represent and reason about emotion-related concepts. It's mechanistic interpretability work, but the practical angle is worth it: understanding how models handle nuance and context actually matters for building better prompts and detecting when models are confabulating.
items.title: Emotion Concepts and Their Function in Large Language Models

items.body: Anthropic is betting big on biotech. Coefficient Bio is a stealth startup, so details are thin, but the size of the deal ($400M in stock) signals that Anthropic sees real opportunity in applying AI to biology and drug discovery. This follows a pattern: AI labs are expanding beyond language into verticals where domain expertise + reasoning = huge value.
items.title: Anthropic Acquires Coefficient Bio for $400M

items.body: Secondary market trading for Anthropic shares is hotter than ever. Meanwhile, OpenAI's trading is cooling. The narrative: Anthropic is seen as having momentum, execution, and diversification (now with biotech). That's real signal in where smart money thinks the race is headed.
items.title: Anthropic Heats Up Private Markets; Hiring, Fundraising, Expanding

items.body: Anthropic rolled out new subscription tiers and is handing out extra usage credits to celebrate. If you've been on the fence about paying for Claude, now's the time to lock in bonus value.
items.title: Claude Pro/Max/Team Launches with Bonus Credits

items.body: Long-form podcast where Andreessen reflects on how AI is reshaping the web, the threat (and potential) of agentic tools like OpenClaw, and why he thinks AI's impact is fundamentally different from previous waves. Worth listening if you're thinking about product strategy or worried about what happens when agents can automate everything.
items.title: Marc Andreessen on the Death of the Browser, AI Agents, and Why This Time Is Different

items.body: A creative case study: using Gemini, NotebookLM, and Google's other AI tools to build an entire multimedia project (YouTube channel + strategy game) in a single afternoon. If you're curious about what's possible with modern tooling, this is a fun listen.
items.title: The Masked Medici: Building YouTube Content + Games with Google AI in One Afternoon

slack_text:
☕ Good Saturday, NOLA! April 4, 2026. It's a quieter day after a wild week, but there's still solid stuff worth your time.

---

🛠 New & Tryable

• <https://apfel.franzai.com|Apfel brings free AI straight to your Mac>. No API keys, no subscriptions — just local models running natively. Dead simple entry point if you've been curious about local inference. <https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47624645|Trending on HN>.

• <https://ctx.rs|ctx is a new agentic development environment> for building smarter AI agents. Handles context awareness, memory, and tool orchestration so you don't have to reinvent it. <https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47626598|Discussion>.

• <https://github.com/borski/travel-hacking-toolkit|Travel Hacking Toolkit>: AI helping you max out frequent flyer points and find better deals. Practical use case that actually saves you money.

---

🔐 Claude Code & Security News

• Claude Code just <https://mtlynch.io/claude-code-found-linux-vulnerability/|found a 23-year-old Linux vulnerability> that humans completely missed. This is the story reshaping how security researchers think about their work. <https://simonwillison.net/2026/Apr/3/vulnerability-research-is-cooked/|Simon's deep-dive on why "vulnerability research is cooked" is essential reading.>

• <https://www.theverge.com/ai-artificial-intelligence/907074/anthropic-openclaw-claude-subscription-ban|Anthropic is making OpenClaw significantly more expensive for Claude users>. Effective today — you can still use it, but it'll cost extra. The security/policy angle is worth thinking about.

---

💭 Research & Thinking

• New research: <https://arstechnica.com/ai/2026/04/research-finds-ai-users-scarily-willing-to-surrender-their-cognition-to-llms/|AI users are willing to abandon logical thinking and just trust wrong answers>>. Not doom — just a design challenge. How do you surface uncertainty and sources so people stay engaged?

• <https://www.latent.space/p/pmarca|Marc Andreessen on the death of the browser, agentic AI, and why this time is different>>. Long but worth it if you're thinking about product strategy in an agent-heavy future.

---

💼 Business Moves

• <https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/03/anthropic-buys-biotech-startup-coefficient-bio-in-400m-deal-reports/|Anthropic just acquired Coefficient Bio for $400M>>. Stealth biotech startup. Signals: AI labs are expanding beyond language into verticals where domain expertise + reasoning = real moat.

• <https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/03/anthropic-is-having-a-moment-in-the-private-markets-spacex-could-spoil-the-party/|Anthropic's on fire in private market trading>> while OpenAI's cooling. Where smart money sees momentum matters.

• GitHub had <https://simonwillison.net/2026/Apr/4/kyle-daigle/#atom-everything|275 million commits last week>>. The AI agent boom is showing up in raw platform activity.
